topic: Unit A2 3: Responding to Music (Set Works and Comparative Study)
subject: music

items.q: What happens at an interrupted cadence?
items.correct: Chord V resolves unexpectedly, usually to chord VI, deceiving the ear which expected the tonic.
items.misconception: An interrupted cadence is when the music suddenly stops with a silence.
items.random_wrong: An interrupted cadence is when the soloist forgets their entry.
items.why: The 'interruption' is harmonic, not literal: the expected V-I resolution is diverted to another chord, which is why it is also called a deceptive cadence.

items.q: Is 'the major key makes it happy and the minor key makes it sad' a sufficient analysis?
items.correct: No - credit comes from precise technical observation: keys, cadences, textures, sonorities and structure, with located examples.
items.misconception: Describing the mood of the music is all an analysis needs.
items.random_wrong: Analysis means copying out the programme note.
items.why: Mood words are conclusions, not evidence; examiners reward the technical detail that explains how the music creates its effect, especially in comparative study.

items.q: What does 'texture' mean when analysing a set work?
items.correct: The relationship between the simultaneous layers of the music, such as monophonic, homophonic or polyphonic.
items.misconception: Texture means how loud or soft the music is.
items.random_wrong: Texture means the material the instruments are made from.
items.why: Loudness is dynamics; texture concerns how many lines there are and how they relate. Confusing the two loses easy marks in listening and essay questions.

items.q: A phrase ending with the chord progression V-I forms which cadence?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Perfect. “Plagal” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An interrupted cadence most typically moves from chord V to which chord?
items.why: Correct answer: VI. “IV” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the strongest evidence to use in a comparative essay on two set works?
items.correct: Specific located musical examples from each work
items.why: It's Specific located musical examples from each work. “General adjectives like 'nice' and 'dramatic'” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A texture made of several independent melodic lines sounding together is called what?
items.why: Correct answer: Contrapuntal (polyphonic). “Monophonic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many dotted-crotchet beats are there in one bar of 9/8?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 beats. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
